2. Merge the feature branch:
git merge feature-branch
3. Delete the feature branch if no longer needed:
git branch -d feature-branch

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is a conflict during merging, and why does it occur?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• A merge conflict occurs when changes in two branches affect the same lines of a file, and Git
cannot determine which changes to keep. This requires manual resolution.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Scenario: How do you handle conflicts during a rebase operation?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Steps:
1. Resolve conflicts in the conflicting files manually.
2. Stage the resolved files:
git add <file>
3. Continue the rebase:
git rebase --continue
4. Abort the rebase if necessary:
git rebase --abort

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What are the benefits of rebasing over merging?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Rebasing creates a linear, cleaner commit history by avoiding merge commits.
• It makes it easier to navigate through history using git log or git blame.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Scenario: How do you create a patch from a commit?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Use git format-patch to create a patch file:
git format-patch -1 <commit-hash>
• Apply the patch:
git apply <patch-file>

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Scenario: How do you squash multiple commits into one before pushing?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Use interactive rebase:
1. Start the rebase for the last n commits:
git rebase -i HEAD~n
2. Replace pick with squash for the commits to squash.
3. Save and exit the editor.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Scenario: How do you handle changes in a forked repository?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Steps:
1. Add the upstream repository:
git remote add upstream <original-repo-url>
2. Fetch the upstream changes:
git fetch upstream
3. Merge the upstream changes into your branch:
git merge upstream/main

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: How do you inspect changes made by another developer?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• View the log of their commits:
git log --author="developer-name"
• See their changes:
git diff <commit-hash>

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Scenario: How do you set a global .gitignore file for all repositories?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Steps:
1. Create a global ignore file:
touch ~/.gitignore_global
2. Configure Git to use it:
git config --global core.excludesfile ~/.gitignore_global

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Scenario: How do you stash changes and switch branches safely?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Steps:
1. Stash the changes:
git stash
2. Switch branches:
git checkout branch-name
3. Reapply the stashed changes:
git stash pop

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is the difference between git stash apply and git stash pop?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• git stash apply: Reapplies the stash but keeps it in the stash list.
• git stash pop: Reapplies the stash and removes it from the stash list.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is Git rerere, and how is it useful?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Git rerere (reuse recorded resolution) remembers how you resolved a conflict so it can
automatically resolve the same conflict in the future.
• Enable rerere:
git config --global rerere.enabled true

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Scenario: How do you view changes in a submodule?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Use:
git diff –submodule
138. Scenario: How do you resolve the error "fatal: refusing to merge unrelated
histories"?
• This error occurs when merging two branches that do not share a common commit history.
• Solution:
1. Use the --allow-unrelated-histories flag:
git merge branch-name --allow-unrelated-histories
139. Scenario: What do you do if Git shows the error "Your local changes would be
overwritten by merge"?
• This error happens when you have uncommitted changes that conflict with the changes
being pulled or merged.
• Solution:
1. Stash your changes:
git stash
2. Perform the merge or pull:
git pull origin branch-name
3. Reapply your changes:
git stash pop
140. Scenario: How do you recover a branch that was accidentally deleted locally and
remotely?
• Steps:
1. Check the reflog for the branch’s last commit:
git reflog
2. Create a new branch from the commit:
git checkout -b branch-name <commit-hash>
3. Push the branch back to the remote repository:
git push origin branch-name

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Scenario: What do you do if a git fetch or git pull is stuck?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Possible solutions:
1. Check your network connection.
2. Add the --verbose flag to debug:
git fetch --verbose
3. Use shallow fetch to minimize data transfer:
git fetch --depth=1

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is the purpose of git ls-tree?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• git ls-tree is used to view the contents of a tree object (e.g., a commit or branch). It lists files
and directories along with their types and SHA-1 hashes.
• Example:
git ls-tree HEAD

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: How do you compare two commits in Git?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Use the git diff command with the two commit hashes:
git diff <commit1> <commit2>

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: How do you find a specific file in the commit history?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Use git log with the file name:
git log -- <file-path>

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is the purpose of git archive?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• git archive is used to create a tar or zip archive of a repository at a specific commit or branch.
• Example:
git archive --format=zip HEAD > archive.zip

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Scenario: How do you set up a branch to track a remote branch?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Use the --set-upstream-to flag:
git branch --set-upstream-to=origin/branch-name

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is the difference between a tracking branch and a local branch?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• A tracking branch is a local branch linked to a remote branch, which makes it easier to pull
and push changes.
• A local branch is any branch that exists only in your local repository.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Scenario: How do you rename a branch locally and on the remote?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Rename locally:
git branch -m old-branch-name new-branch-name
• Rename remotely:
1. Delete the old remote branch:
git push origin --delete old-branch-name
2. Push the new branch:
git push origin new-branch-name

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Scenario: How do you cancel an incomplete merge?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Use the git merge --abort command:
git merge --abort

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is the difference between a fast-forward merge and a three-way merge?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Fast-forward merge: Occurs when the branch being merged has not diverged, and Git can
simply move the HEAD pointer forward.
• Three-way merge: Used when branches have diverged, requiring Git to create a new merge
commit.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Scenario: How do you perform a no-commit merge?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Use the --no-commit flag:
git merge --no-commit branch-name

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What are the risks of rebasing a public branch?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Rebasing rewrites history, which can lead to conflicts and issues for collaborators who are
also working on the same branch. It should only be done on private branches.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Scenario: How do you edit an old commit message during a rebase?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Steps:
1. Start an interactive rebase:
git rebase -i HEAD~n
2. Replace pick with reword for the desired commit.
3. Edit the message when prompted.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: How do you perform a rebase and automatically resolve conflicts in favor of one branch?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Use the --strategy-option=theirs flag:
git rebase -s recursive -X theirs branch-name
155. Scenario: How do you revert changes in a specific commit while keeping later
changes intact?
• Use git revert:
git revert <commit-hash>

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is a Git hook, and how can you implement one?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• A Git hook is a script that runs automatically during specific Git events (e.g., pre-commit,
post-merge).
• Example: Adding a pre-commit hook:
1. Create a file .git/hooks/pre-commit:
#!/bin/sh
echo "Running pre-commit hook"
2. Make it executable:
chmod +x .git/hooks/pre-commit

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Scenario: How do you handle a force-push on a shared branch?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Steps:
1. Fetch the latest changes:
git fetch origin
2. Reset your local branch:
git reset --hard origin/branch-name

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: How do you fetch changes for a single file from a remote branch?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Use git checkout with the branch and file path:
git checkout origin/branch-name -- <file-path>

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Scenario: How do you create multiple stashes with custom messages?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Use the git stash save command with a message:
git stash save "Stash message 1"
git stash save "Stash message 2"

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: How do you apply a stash without removing it from the stash list?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Use:
git stash apply stash@{n}

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Scenario: How do you split a repository into two smaller repositories?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Use git filter-repo:
1. Install the tool:
pip install git-filter-repo
2. Split the repository:
git filter-repo --path subfolder-name --force

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is the purpose of the git worktree command?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• The git worktree command allows you to work on multiple branches in the same repository
without switching branches.
• Example:
git worktree add ../new-worktree branch-name

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Scenario: How do you fix "fatal: Authentication failed" when using HTTPS?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• This error often occurs due to incorrect credentials or token expiration.
• Solutions:
1. Update your credentials:
git credential-cache exit
Then re-enter your credentials on the next pull or push.
2. Use a personal access token (PAT) instead of a password if the service requires it:
▪ Generate a PAT from your Git hosting platform (e.g., GitHub, GitLab).
▪ Use the PAT as the password when prompted.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Scenario: How do you fix "index.lock" errors when performing Git operations?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• This error occurs if a Git process was interrupted, leaving a lock file behind.
• Solution:
1. Verify no Git processes are running:
ps aux | grep git
2. Remove the lock file:
rm -f .git/index.lock

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Scenario: What do you do if a commit has the wrong author information?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Use git commit --amend to correct the author for the last commit:
git commit --amend --author="Name <email@example.com>"
• For multiple commits, use:
git rebase -i HEAD~n
Then edit each commit’s author.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Scenario: How do you troubleshoot "detected inconsistent line endings in file"?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Configure Git to handle line endings:
1. Set core.autocrlf for your platform:
▪ On Windows:
git config --global core.autocrlf true
▪ On Linux/macOS:
git config --global core.autocrlf input
2. Normalize the file’s line endings:
git add --renormalize .
git commit -m "Normalize line endings"

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What does git reflog do, and how can it help recover lost commits?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• git reflog tracks changes to HEAD, allowing you to recover commits that are no longer
reachable via branches.
• Example:
1. List the reflog:
git reflog
2. Recover a lost commit:
git checkout <commit-hash>

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is the purpose of git cherry-pick?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• git cherry-pick applies a specific commit from one branch to another without merging the
entire branch.
• Example:
git cherry-pick <commit-hash>

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: How do you view all files in a specific commit?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Use:
git show --name-only <commit-hash>

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is the difference between git log and git show?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• git log: Shows a history of commits.
• git show: Displays detailed information about a specific commit, including changes made.
171. Scenario: How do you merge changes from a specific branch into your branch
without a full merge?
• Use git cherry-pick to apply specific commits:
git cherry-pick <commit-hash>

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Scenario: How do you delete a remote branch?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Command:
git push origin --delete branch-name

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is the difference between git branch and git rev-parse?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• git branch: Lists, creates, or deletes branches.
• git rev-parse: Converts branch names or references into their SHA-1 hashes.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Scenario: How do you avoid creating a merge commit for trivial changes?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Use a fast-forward merge by ensuring the branch has no divergent changes:
git merge --ff-only branch-name
175. Scenario: How do you force a merge commit even when a fast-forward merge is
possible?
• Use the --no-ff flag:
git merge --no-ff branch-name

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Scenario: How do you merge only specific files from another branch?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Steps:
1. Check out the files:
git checkout branch-name -- file-path
2. Stage and commit the changes:
git add file-path
git commit -m "Merged specific file from branch-name"

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Scenario: How do you interactively rebase to reorder commits?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Steps:
1. Start the interactive rebase:
git rebase -i HEAD~n
2. Change the order of the commits in the editor.
3. Save and exit.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Scenario: What do you do if a rebase introduces a bug?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Abort the rebase and return to the pre-rebase state:
git rebase --abort

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is the difference between rebasing and cherry-picking?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Rebasing: Reapplies a series of commits onto a new base, modifying commit history.
• Cherry-picking: Applies specific commits to another branch without altering history.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Scenario: How do you work on a hotfix in Gitflow?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Steps:
1. Create a hotfix branch:
git checkout -b hotfix/fix-name main
2. Make changes and commit them.
3. Merge the hotfix into main and develop:
git checkout main
git merge hotfix/fix-name
git checkout develop
git merge hotfix/fix-name

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What are Git tags used for in workflows?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Tags mark specific points in history, often for releases.
• Example:
git tag -a v1.0 -m "Version 1.0"
git push origin v1.0

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Scenario: How do you resolve conflicts when merging a pull request?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Steps:
1. Pull the PR locally:
git fetch origin pull/<PR-number>/head:pr-branch
2. Switch to the branch and resolve conflicts manually.
3. Push the resolved branch:
git push origin pr-branch

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: How do you create a pull request in GitHub using Git commands?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Push a branch:
git push origin branch-name
• Use the GitHub CLI to create a PR:
gh pr create --title "PR Title" --body "PR Description"

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Scenario: How do you stash changes for a specific file only?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Command:
git stash push <file-path>

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Scenario: How do you preview what a stash contains before applying it?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Command:
git stash show stash@{n} --patch

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Scenario: How do you manage submodules in a large repository?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Add a submodule:
git submodule add <repo-url> <path>
• Update submodules:
git submodule update --init --recursive

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is the purpose of the git fsck command?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• git fsck checks the integrity of a Git repository.
• Example:
git fsck
188. Scenario: How do you handle the "detached HEAD" state after checking out a
commit directly?
• If you want to create a new branch from this state:
git checkout -b new-branch
• If you want to return to an existing branch:
git checkout branch-name

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Scenario: What do you do if you accidentally staged changes to the wrong file?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Unstage the file:
git reset <file-path>
• Make the necessary adjustments and restage the correct file(s):
git add correct-file-path
190. Scenario: How do you resolve "fatal: origin does not appear to be a Git
repository"?
• This error occurs when the remote URL is missing or incorrect.
• Solution:
1. Verify the remote:
git remote -v
2. Add or correct the remote URL:
git remote add origin <repository-url>

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Scenario: How do you fix the "merge failed" error when rebasing?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Steps:
1. Identify conflicting files:
git status
2. Resolve conflicts manually.
3. Stage the resolved files:
git add <file>
4. Continue the rebase:
git rebase --continue

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: How do you count the number of commits in a branch?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Command:
git rev-list --count branch-name

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What does the HEAD^ and HEAD~n syntax mean?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• HEAD^: Refers to the immediate parent of the current commit.
• HEAD~n: Refers to the nth parent commit from the current commit.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: How do you display the current branch name?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Command:
git branch --show-current

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: How do you display detailed information about a specific commit?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Command:
git show <commit-hash>

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Scenario: How do you ensure no one pushes directly to main?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Set up branch protection rules on your Git hosting platform (e.g., GitHub or GitLab).
• Alternatively, you can create a server-side pre-receive hook:
#!/bin/sh
if [ "$GIT_BRANCH" = "main" ]; then
echo "Direct pushes to main are not allowed!"
exit 1
fi

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Scenario: How do you archive a feature branch that is no longer in use?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Steps:
1. Push the branch to the remote if not already done:
git push origin feature-branch
2. Delete it locally:
git branch -d feature-branch
3. Move it to an archive remote repository, if needed.
198. Scenario: How do you identify and merge only the commits that add a specific file
or feature?
• Use git log to identify the commits:
git log -- <file-path>
• Cherry-pick those commits:
git cherry-pick <commit-hash>

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Scenario: How do you merge branches when there are too many conflicts?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Steps:
1. Perform a merge:
git merge branch-name
2. Use a merge tool to simplify conflict resolution:
git mergetool
3. Resolve the conflicts manually if necessary and commit:
git commit

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Scenario: How do you track changes made to Git submodules?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
• Commands:
1. Update the submodule:
git submodule update --remote
2. Commit the submodule changes:
git add <submodule-path>
git commit -m "Updated submodule"

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is Git?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
Git is a distributed version control system (DVCS) that helps
track code changes, collaborate with teams, roll back to older
versions, and manage branching/merging.
💡
Pro Tip: Always mention "distributed" — it highlights Git’s offline
capability.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is GitHub?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
GitHub is a cloud platform to host Git repositories and
collaborate using Pull Requests, Issues, Actions (CI/CD), and project
boards.
💡
Pro Tip: Say Git = tool, GitHub = platform.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Common Git commands?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
git init → Initialize a new Git repository
git clone <url> → Copy repository from remote to local
git status → Show modified/untracked files
git add . → Stage all changes
git commit -m "msg" → Save a snapshot with a message
git log --oneline → Show commit history in short form
git branch → List branches
git checkout -b <branch> → Create + switch to a new branch
git merge <branch> → Merge branch into current
git push origin main → Push local commits to GitHub
git pull → Fetch + merge changes from remote
git fetch → Only download changes (no merge)
💡
Pro Tip: In interviews, mention git commit -am "msg" as a shortcut
for tracked files.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Difference between Git and GitHub?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
Git → Local VCS tool that manages versions of code.
GitHub → Cloud hosting service that extends Git with collaboration
features.
Interview Edge: Mention alternatives like GitLab, Bitbucket.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is a repository?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
A repository is a project directory managed by Git,
containing code, commit history, and branches.
Local repository → Exists on your machine (git init).
Remote repository → Hosted on GitHub for team collaboration.
💡
Pro Tip: Interviewers like when you highlight the difference
between local and remote repos.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is a commit?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
A commit is a snapshot of code at a point in time, identified
by a unique SHA hash, author, timestamp, and commit message.
💡
Pro Tip: A clear commit message helps track why a change was
made.
📌
Section 2: Branching & Merging

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is a branch?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
A branch is a parallel line of development. It allows
developers to build features without affecting the main branch.
💡
Pro Tip: Mention how feature branches prevent breaking
production code.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is a merge conflict? How do you resolve it?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
A merge conflict happens when two branches modify the
same code section. Git cannot decide which change to keep.
Steps:
1. Open file and check conflict markers.
2. Decide correct changes (or combine).
3. Save → git add → git commit.
💡
Pro Tip: Mention that handling conflicts quickly is vital in team
projects.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Difference between git merge and git rebase?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
Merge → Combines histories, keeps non-linear commits.
Rebase → Replays commits on another branch, making history
linear.
💡
Pro Tip: Teams often use merge for collaboration, and rebase
for personal branches.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is a fast-forward merge?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
A fast-forward merge occurs when no new commits exist on
the target branch, so Git simply moves the branch pointer ahead
without creating a merge commit.
💡
Pro Tip: Fast-forward keeps history simple, but doesn’t show that a
branch existed.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is a detached HEAD?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
Detached HEAD occurs when Git points directly to a commit
instead of a branch.
Commits made here won’t belong to any branch unless you create
one.
💡
Pro Tip: Used for experiments or reviewing older commits.
📌
Section 3: Commands

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is the difference between git pull and git fetch?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
git fetch downloads commits from remote into remote-tracking
branches (like origin/main) but doesn’t merge them.
git pull does fetch + merge, updating your current branch
automatically.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is git stash?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
git stash temporarily saves uncommitted changes and resets your working
directory.
Example: You are mid-feature but must switch to main to fix a bug. Run git
stash, switch branch, fix and commit the bug, then return and restore with
git stash pop.
📌
Section 4: Staging & Workflow

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is git add and why do we need it?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
git add stages changes from the working directory to the
staging area. Git uses a 3-stage system: Working Directory → Staging
Area → Repository.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Do we always need git add . before commit?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
No. For tracked files, you can commit directly with git
commit -am "msg". But for new/untracked files, git add is mandatory.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is git stash and when is it useful?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
git stash temporarily saves uncommitted work and clears the
working directory.
Example: In the middle of a feature, you need to switch to main to fix
a bug → run git stash, later use git stash pop to restore.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is .gitignore?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
A file that lists files/folders Git should ignore (e.g., logs, build
files, environment secrets).

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Difference between git reset, git revert, and git checkout?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
reset → Moves HEAD pointer back, possibly deleting commits.
revert → Safely creates a new commit that undoes changes.
checkout → Switches branches or restores files.
💡
Pro Tip: Say “I prefer revert in teams because it preserves
history.”
📌
Section 5: Remote Operations

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is a remote in Git?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
A remote is a reference to a hosted repository (usually on
GitHub). Default name = origin.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: How do you push a branch to GitHub?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
git push origin branch-name
For first time:
git push -u origin branch-name
(-u sets upstream tracking for future pushes).

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is the difference between fork and clone?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
Fork → Copies repository to your GitHub account.
Clone → Copies repository to your local machine.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is a Pull Request (PR)?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
A PR is a request to merge code into another branch on
GitHub. It allows code review, discussion, and approval before
merging.
📌
Section 6: Tags & Releases

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is a Git tag?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
A tag is a label pointing to a specific commit, often used for
releases (v1.0.0).

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Difference between lightweight and annotated tags?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
Lightweight → Simple pointer.
Annotated → Stores metadata like tagger, date, and message
(preferred for releases).

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: How do you create and push a tag?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
git tag -a v1.0.0 -m "First release"
git push origin v1.0.0

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: How do you delete a tag?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
Local: git tag -d v1.0.0
Remote: git push --delete origin v1.0.0

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: How to see commits between two tags?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
git log v1.0.0..v2.0.0 --oneline
📌
Section 7: Advanced & Best Practices

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is GitHub Actions?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
GitHub Actions is a CI/CD automation tool inside GitHub. It
allows workflows like build, test, and deployment triggered on events
(push, PR, etc.).

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is GitHub Flow vs Git Flow?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
GitHub Flow → Simple strategy: create branch → PR → merge into
main.
Git Flow → Structured: uses main, develop, feature, release, hotfix
branches.
💡
Pro Tip: Mention GitHub Flow for startups, Git Flow for
enterprise projects.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: How do you handle large files in GitHub?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
By using Git LFS (Large File Storage), which stores large
binaries outside normal Git history.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is the difference between main and origin/main?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
main → Local branch.
origin/main → Remote-tracking branch (state of remote main).

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: Best practices with Git in teams?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
Write clear commit messages.
Use branches for new features.
Keep main protected.
Resolve merge conflicts early.
Use PRs for review.
💡
Pro Tip: Add “We also use GitHub Actions for CI/CD” to show
awareness of modern workflows.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What happens when you run git fetch?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
git fetch downloads new changes from a remote repository, but does not apply them to
your local branch.
It updates your remote-tracking branches so that they match the state of the corresponding branches
on the remote repository.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is the difference between git merge and git rebase?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
git merge combines changes from one branch into another branch, creating a new merge
commit that has two parent commits.
git rebase, on the other hand, rewrites the history of a branch by moving the branch to the tip of another
branch and applying the changes in a linear fashion.
This creates a cleaner, easier-to-understand commit history, but can lead to conflicts if multiple
people are working on the same branch.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: You have made a commit that contains sensitive information, such as a password, and you want to remove it from the Git repository. What is the best way to do this?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
The best way to remove a commit that contains sensitive information from the Git
repository is to use the git filter-branch command.
First, use the git log command to identify the commit that contains the sensitive information.
Then, use the git filter-branch --force --index-filter 'git rm --cached --ignore-unmatch path/to/file'
-- prune-empty --tag-name-filter cat- -all command to remove the sensitive information from the
commit and all subsequent commits.
Finally, use the git push --force command to update the remote repository.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: How do you resolve conflicts in Git?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
To resolve conflicts in Git, you first need to identify which files have conflicts by running git
status.
Then, open the conflicted files in a text editor and look for the sections marked with <<<<<<<,
=======, and >>>>>>>.
Edit the files to remove the conflict markers and make the necessary changes to resolve the conflicts.
Once you have resolved all conflicts, stage the changes with git add and commit the changes with git
commit.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is the difference between git push and git push –force?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
git push sends your local changes to a remote repository and updates the remote branches
to match your local branches. git push --force, however, overwrites the remote branches with your
local branches, even if they have diverged.
This can cause you to lose changes if you are not careful, so it should be used with caution.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is the difference between git reset and git revert?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
git reset removes commits from the current branch, moving the branch pointer back to an
earlier commit.
This can be useful if you want to undo local changes that have not been pushed to a remote
repository. git revert, on the other hand, creates a new commit that undoes the changes made by a
previous commit.
This is useful if you want to undo changes that have already been pushed to a remote repository and
shared with others.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: You have made a mistake in a commit message and want to change it. What is the best way to do this?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
The best way to change a commit message in Git is to use the git commit --amend command.
First, use the git log command to identify the commit with the incorrect message. Then, use the git
commit --amend -m "corrected message" command to change the commit message.
Finally, use the git push --force command to update the remote repository.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is Git reflow?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
Git reflow is a tool that simplifies Git workflows by automating the creation of pull
requests and other common Git tasks.
It provides a set of commands that allow developers to focus on writing code, rather than managing Git
branches and pull requests.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is the difference between Git and GitHub?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
Git is a distributed version control system that allows developers to track changes in
source code over time.
GitHub, on the other hand, is a web-based platform that provides hosting for Git repositories, as well as
additional features such as issue tracking, code review, and collaboration tools.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is a Git hook?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
A Git hook is a script that runs automatically before or after a Git command is executed.
Hooks can be used to perform tasks such as checking for code quality, enforcing commit message
formatting, and sending notifications.
Git provides several pre-defined hooks, and developers can also create their own custom hooks.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: You have accidentally deleted a file that was committed to your Git repository. What is the best way to restore the file?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
The best way to restore a deleted file in Git is to use the git checkout
command. First, identify the commit in which the file was last present using the git log
command.
Then, use the git checkout <commit> -- <file> command to restore the file from the specified commit.
If the file was deleted in the most recent commit, you can use the git reset HEAD~1 command to
reset the branch to the previous commit, use git checkout to restore the file, and then use the git add
and git commit commands to commit the restored file.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is Git bisect?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
Git bisect is a tool that helps you find the commit that introduced a bug. It works by doing a
binary search of the commit history, asking you to test a commit in the middle of the range each time.
Based on your feedback, it will narrow down the range of possible commits until it finds the one that
introduced the bug.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is a Git stash?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
A Git stash is a way to temporarily save changes that you are not ready to commit, so that you
can work on a different branch or pull in changes from another repository.
Stashing your changes creates a new "stash" object that stores your changes, and you can apply the
stash later using git stash apply or git stash pop.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: You have made a commit to a branch, but you want to move it to a different branch. What is the best way to do this?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
The best way to move a commit to a different branch in Git is to use the git cherry-pick
command.
First, identify the commit hash using the git log command. Then, switch to the target branch using
the git checkout command.
Finally, use the git cherry-pick <commit> command to apply the commit to the target branch.
This will create a new commit with the changes from the original commit applied to the target branch.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is Git LFS?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
Git LFS (Large File Storage) is an extension for Git that allows you to manage large files
such as audio, video, and graphics, that Git cannot handle efficiently.
Instead of storing the large files in the Git repository, Git LFS stores a pointer to the large file in the
repository, and the actual file is stored in a separate Git LFS server.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is a Git submodule?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
A Git submodule is a way to include a separate Git repository as a subdirectory of another
repository.
This is useful when you want to include a library or other code from a separate repository, without
copying the code into your repository.
The submodule is stored as a pointer to a specific commit in the separate repository, so that other
developers can update the submodule to the latest version of the code.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is Git cherry-pick?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
Git cherry-pick is a command that allows you to apply a specific commit from one branch to
another branch.
This is useful when you want to apply a bugfix or feature from one branch to another branch without
merging the entire branch.
The cherry-picked commit is treated as a new commit, so it will have a new commit ID.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: You have made some changes to a file, but you want to see what the file looked like before the changes. What is the best way to view the previous version of the file?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
The best way to view the previous version of a file in Git is to use the git show command.
First, use the git log command to identify the commit in which the file was last modified.
Then, use the git show <commit>:path/to/file command to view the contents of the file at the specified
commit.
If the file has been modified multiple times, you can use the git log --oneline path/to/file command to
identify the commit hash for the previous version of the file, and then use the git show <commit>
command to view the contents of the file at the specified commit.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is Git reflog?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
Git reflog is a log of all the changes to Git references (such as branches, tags, and
HEAD) that have occurred in a repository.
It can be used to recover lost commits, branches, or tags that have been deleted or overwritten.
The reflog can be accessed using the git reflog command.

Git & GitHub / GitLab.question: What is Git filter-branch?
Git & GitHub / GitLab.answer:
Git filter-branch is a command that allows you to rewrite the history of a Git repository.
It can be used to remove sensitive data (such as passwords or API keys) from the commit history, or to
split a large repository into smaller repositories.
The filter-branch command works by applying a set of filters to each commit in the repository, and
creating new commits with the filtered changes.

SonarQube & Code Quality.question: What is SonarQube, and why is it used?
SonarQube & Code Quality.answer:
SonarQube is an open-source platform used to continuously inspect the
•
code quality of projects by detecting bugs, vulnerabilities, and code
smells. It supports multiple programming languages and integrates well
with CI/CD pipelines, enabling teams to improve code quality through
static analysis.
It provides reports on code duplication, test coverage, security hotspots,
•
and code maintainability.

SonarQube & Code Quality.question: What are SonarQube Quality Gates?
SonarQube & Code Quality.answer:
A Quality Gate is a set of conditions that must be met for a project to be
•
considered good in terms of code quality. It’s based on metrics such as
bugs, vulnerabilities, code coverage, code duplication, etc.
The pipeline can be configured to fail if the project does not meet the
•
defined quality gate conditions, preventing poor-quality code from being
released.

SonarQube & Code Quality.question: What is a ‘code smell’ in SonarQube?
SonarQube & Code Quality.answer:
A code smell is a maintainability issue in the code that may not
•
necessarily result in bugs or security vulnerabilities but makes the code
harder to read, maintain, or extend. Examples include long methods, too
many parameters in a function, or poor variable naming conventions.

SonarQube & Code Quality.question: What is the difference between bugs, vulnerabilities, and code smells in SonarQube?
SonarQube & Code Quality.answer:
Bugs: Issues in the code that are likely to cause incorrect or unexpected
•
behavior during execution.
Vulnerabilities: Security risks that can make your application susceptible
•
to attacks (e.g., SQL injections, cross-site scripting).
Code Smells: Maintainability issues that don't necessarily lead to
•
immediate errors but make the code more difficult to work with in the
long term (e.g., poor variable names, large methods).

SonarQube & Code Quality.question: What are SonarQube issues, and how are they categorized?
SonarQube & Code Quality.answer:
SonarQube issues are problems found in the code, categorized into three
•
severity levels:
1. Blocker: Issues that can cause the program to fail (e.g., bugs,
security vulnerabilities).
2. Critical: Significant problems that could lead to unexpected
behavior.
3. Minor: Less severe issues, often related to coding style or best
practices.

SonarQube & Code Quality.question: How does SonarQube help manage technical debt?
SonarQube & Code Quality.answer:
SonarQube calculates technical debt as the estimated time required to fix
•
all code quality issues (bugs, vulnerabilities, code smells).
This helps teams prioritize what should be refactored, fixed, or improved,
•
and balance this with feature development.

SonarQube & Code Quality.question: How does SonarQube handle multiple branches in a project?
SonarQube & Code Quality.answer:
SonarQube has a branch analysis feature that allows you to analyze
•
different branches of your project and track the evolution of code quality
in each branch.
This is helpful in DevOps pipelines to ensure that new feature branches or
•
hotfixes meet the same code quality standards as the main branch.

SonarQube & Code Quality.question: What is SonarLint, and how does it relate to SonarQube?
SonarQube & Code Quality.answer:
SonarLint is a plugin that integrates with IDEs (like IntelliJ IDEA,
•
Eclipse, VSCode) to provide real-time code analysis. It helps developers
find and fix issues in their code before committing them.
SonarLint complements SonarQube by giving developers instant
•
feedback in their local development environments.

SonarQube & Code Quality.question: What are some best practices when using SonarQube in a CI/CD pipeline?
SonarQube & Code Quality.answer:
Automate the quality gate checks: Set up pipelines to fail if the quality
•
gate is not met.
Ensure code coverage: Aim for a high percentage of test coverage to
•
detect untested and potentially buggy code.
Regular analysis: Analyze your project code frequently, preferably on
•
every commit or pull request.
Use quality profiles: Customize quality profiles to match your team's
•
coding standards.
Fix critical issues first: Prioritize fixing bugs and vulnerabilities over
•
code smells.

SonarQube & Code Quality.question: What is the SonarQube Scanner, and how is it used?
SonarQube & Code Quality.answer:
The SonarQube Scanner is a tool that analyzes the source code and
•
sends the results to the SonarQube server for further processing.
It can be run as part of a CI/CD pipeline or manually using the command
•
line. The basic command is sonar-scanner, and you need to provide the
necessary project and server details in the configuration file (sonar-
project.properties).
Combined (GitHub Actions, ArgoCD,
Kubernetes):

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is DevSecOps, and how is it different from DevOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
DevSecOps integrates security practices within the DevOps lifecycle, emphasizing automation and
collaboration. While DevOps focuses on rapid development and delivery of applications, DevSecOps
ensures security is part of every stage — from planning to deployment. This shift-left approach
ensures vulnerabilities are caught early.

DevSecOps & Security.question: Why is shifting security left in DevSecOps important?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
Shifting security left involves incorporating security earlier in the software development lifecycle
(SDLC). It reduces the cost and impact of security vulnerabilities, as issues detected later in the cycle
are more expensive and harder to fix.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What are the core practices of DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
• Automation: Security checks are automated within CI/CD pipelines.
• Continuous Monitoring: Systems are continuously monitored for vulnerabilities.
• Collaboration: Development, security, and operations teams work closely.
• Shift-left Testing: Security testing is integrated early in the SDLC.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What tools can be used for code security scanning in DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
Common tools include:
• SonarQube for static code analysis.
• Trivy and Anchore for container vulnerability scanning.
• OWASP Dependency-Check for checking vulnerable libraries.
• Snyk and Checkmarx for identifying code vulnerabilities.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What are OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
The OWASP Top 10 list highlights the most critical security risks to web applications:
• Injection
• Broken Authentication
• Sensitive Data Exposure
DevOps Shack
• XML External Entities (XXE)
• Broken Access Control
• Security Misconfiguration
• Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
• Insecure Deserialization
• Using Components with Known Vulnerabilities
• Insufficient Logging & Monitoring

DevSecOps & Security.question: How do you integrate security in a CI/CD pipeline?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
Integrating security involves:
• Static Application Security Testing (SAST): Scans the codebase for vulnerabilities.
• Dynamic Application Security Testing (DAST): Scans the running application.
• Container Security: Ensures containers are free of vulnerabilities before deployment.
• Infrastructure as Code (IaC) Scanning: Tools like Terraform or CloudFormation should be
checked for misconfigurations.

DevSecOps & Security.question: Explain the role of SAST in DevSecOps. SAST (Static Application Security Testing) is a method of analyzing source code for security vulnerabilities without executing the program. It helps developers identify potential vulnerabilities during coding, reducing risks before deployment
What is a DAST tool, and why is it used in DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
DAST (Dynamic Application Security Testing) is used to test running applications for vulnerabilities by
simulating attacks, such as SQL Injection or XSS. Unlike SAST, DAST doesn’t have access to the code
and interacts with the application in a black-box manner.

DevSecOps & Security.question: Can you name some popular DAST tools?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
Popular DAST tools include:
• OWASP ZAP (Zed Attack Proxy)
• Burp Suite
DevOps Shack
• Netsparker
• Acunetix

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is the difference between SAST and DAST?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
• SAST: Scans source code and finds issues early in the development cycle (shift-left).
• DAST: Scans running applications, finding issues post-deployment or in the later stages of
testing.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is Trivy, and how is it used in DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
Trivy is a vulnerability scanner for container images, file systems, and Git repositories. It scans for
vulnerabilities in OS packages and application dependencies and is often used in CI/CD pipelines to
automate security checks.

DevSecOps & Security.question: Explain the concept of Infrastructure as Code (IaC). Infrastructure as Code (IaC) is the practice of managing and provisioning infrastructure through code rather than manual processes. Tools like Terraform, AWS CloudFormation, and Ansible allow for automated and consistent provisioning of infrastructure, improving scalability, and minimizing human errors
What is the role of container security in DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
Container security ensures that containers, such as Docker images, are free from vulnerabilities. This
includes scanning for vulnerabilities in base images, preventing container drift, securing container
registries, and running containers with minimal privileges.
DevOps Shack

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is the difference between Symmetric and Asymmetric encryption?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
• Symmetric Encryption: Uses a single key for both encryption and decryption (e.g., AES).
• Asymmetric Encryption: Uses a public key for encryption and a private key for decryption
(e.g., RSA).

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is the role of encryption in DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
Encryption ensures that sensitive data (in transit and at rest) is protected from unauthorized access.
In DevSecOps, encryption mechanisms are implemented at various stages, such as encrypting secrets
in CI/CD pipelines or using TLS for secure communication.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What are secrets management tools, and why are they important?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
Secrets management tools securely store and manage sensitive information like passwords, API keys,
and tokens. Tools like HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, and Azure Key Vault are critical in
ensuring that sensitive data is not hard-coded in scripts or configurations.

DevSecOps & Security.question: How do you manage secrets in a CI/CD pipeline?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
• Store secrets in secret management tools like Vault or AWS Secrets Manager.
• Never hard-code secrets in the repository.
• Use environment variables to inject secrets during runtime.
• Ensure that secrets are encrypted and rotated regularly.
DevOps Shack

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is SonarQube, and how does it help in DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
SonarQube is a code quality and security analysis tool that helps in identifying bugs, vulnerabilities,
and code smells. It integrates with CI/CD pipelines to automatically check the code after every build
and before deployment, ensuring code security and quality.

DevSecOps & Security.question: Explain how you can use Jenkins to implement security scans in a CI/CD pipeline. Jenkins can integrate with security tools such as SonarQube, OWASP Dependency-Check, Trivy, and Anchore to automate security scanning. These scans can be added as pipeline stages, ensuring that each code commit is scanned before it's deployed to production
What is the importance of API security in DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
API security ensures that data exchanged between services is protected. This includes securing API
endpoints, using authentication and authorization (OAuth2, JWT), rate-limiting, and monitoring for
malicious activities like injection attacks or DDoS.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is the OWASP API Security Top 10?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
The OWASP API Security Top 10 highlights the most common security risks for APIs, such as:
• Broken Object Level Authorization
• Excessive Data Exposure
• Lack of Resources & Rate Limiting
• Broken Function Level Authorization
• Mass Assignment

DevSecOps & Security.question: How do you secure a Kubernetes cluster in a DevSecOps environment?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
To secure a Kubernetes cluster:
• Use Role-Based Access Control (RBAC).
• Implement network policies to control pod communication.
• Use encrypted communication (TLS).
• Regularly scan container images.
DevOps Shack
• Ensure only signed and trusted images are deployed.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What are Kubernetes network policies, and how do they enhance security?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
Kubernetes network policies control the traffic between pods, services, and the outside world. By
restricting communication to only what's necessary, you can reduce the attack surface and prevent
unauthorized access within the cluster.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is RBAC in Kubernetes, and why is it important in a DevSecOps setup?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) in Kubernetes restricts access to resources based on the roles
assigned to users or applications. It enhances security by ensuring that only authorized users can
perform specific actions on Kubernetes resources, reducing the risk of misconfigurations or malicious
actions.

DevSecOps & Security.question: How do you implement RBAC in Kubernetes?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
RBAC in Kubernetes is implemented using Roles, ClusterRoles, RoleBindings, and ClusterRoleBindings.
These resources define who can perform which actions on specific resources. For example, a Role
can grant permissions to perform get or list operations on Pods, and a RoleBinding assigns that role
to a user or group.

DevSecOps & Security.question: How does SonarQube help improve code security and quality in a DevSecOps pipeline?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
SonarQube scans source code to identify code quality issues, bugs, and security vulnerabilities. It
helps ensure that code meets defined standards, is secure, and performs efficiently. By integrating
SonarQube into a CI/CD pipeline, teams can automatically scan code after each commit and enforce
quality gates before deploying to production.

DevSecOps & Security.question: How do you manage vulnerabilities in third-party dependencies?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
• Use tools like OWASP Dependency-Check, Snyk, and Black Duck to scan third-party
dependencies for known vulnerabilities.
• Regularly update dependencies to their latest, secure versions.
• Implement monitoring systems to receive alerts for newly discovered vulnerabilities in the
libraries you're using.
DevOps Shack

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is OWASP Dependency-Check, and how does it work?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
OWASP Dependency-Check is a tool that identifies vulnerabilities in project dependencies. It works
by scanning dependencies (e.g., JAR, NPM packages) and comparing them against known
vulnerability databases (e.g., NVD - National Vulnerability Database).

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is the role of threat modeling in DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
Threat modeling helps teams proactively identify, assess, and mitigate potential security threats to
an application or infrastructure. By integrating threat modeling into the early stages of the SDLC,
DevSecOps teams can design secure systems and reduce the risk of vulnerabilities being exploited in
production.

DevSecOps & Security.question: Explain what a security misconfiguration is and how you can prevent it. A security misconfiguration occurs when systems or applications are configured with insecure settings, leaving them vulnerable to attacks. Prevention includes: • Regular audits and configuration reviews. • Automating configuration management with tools like Ansible or Terraform. • Enforcing best practices like disabling unused features and services
What is an Infrastructure as Code (IaC) vulnerability?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
An IaC vulnerability refers to security risks introduced through misconfigured or insecure IaC
templates (e.g., Terraform, CloudFormation). These can include open S3 buckets, excessive
permissions in security groups, or unencrypted resources.

DevSecOps & Security.question: How do you secure IaC templates?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
• Use tools like Checkov, TFLint, or Terraform Sentinel to scan IaC templates for security
misconfigurations.
• Enforce least privilege for resources like IAM roles or security groups.
• Regularly update IaC templates to follow the latest security best practices.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is the principle of least privilege, and how does it apply to DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
DevOps Shack
The principle of least privilege ensures that users, systems, and applications only have the minimal
level of access necessary to perform their tasks. In DevSecOps, it applies to:
• IAM Roles: Granting minimal permissions to services and users.
• Kubernetes RBAC: Restricting access to specific resources.
• Secrets Management: Ensuring only authorized entities can access sensitive data.

DevSecOps & Security.question: How do you handle security in serverless applications?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
• Ensure functions have minimal permissions.
• Regularly scan serverless dependencies for vulnerabilities.
• Secure event sources (e.g., API Gateway) and enforce authentication and authorization.
• Monitor functions for anomalous behaviors using logging and monitoring tools like AWS
CloudWatch.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is a security token, and how is it used in DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
A security token is a digital credential used to authenticate a user or system to access resources. In
DevSecOps, security tokens are often used for API access, continuous delivery pipelines, and third-
party integrations, ensuring secure and automated operations.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What are container runtime security best practices?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
• Run containers as non-root users.
• Use signed and trusted base images.
• Regularly scan images for vulnerabilities.
• Implement resource limits (CPU, memory) to prevent abuse.
• Isolate containers using namespaces and cgroups.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is image signing in Docker, and how does it work?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
Image signing ensures that only trusted images are deployed in a containerized environment. Docker
Content Trust (DCT) can be used to sign images. When enabled, Docker verifies the signature before
pulling or running an image, preventing unverified images from being used.
DevOps Shack

DevSecOps & Security.question: What are common security concerns when using CI/CD pipelines?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
• Exposing secrets or credentials within pipelines.
• Unsecured artifact repositories that can lead to supply chain attacks.
• Insecure source code integration where code is not validated for vulnerabilities.
• Unrestricted access to production environments.

DevSecOps & Security.question: How can you secure secrets in a CI/CD pipeline?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
• Use secret management tools like Vault or AWS Secrets Manager.
• Never hard-code secrets into repositories.
• Inject secrets dynamically as environment variables during pipeline execution.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What are the risks of using public container images, and how do you mitigate them?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
Public container images may contain known vulnerabilities or malicious software. To mitigate risks:
• Always scan images for vulnerabilities.
• Use trusted sources or create base images internally.
• Regularly update and patch container images.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is a supply chain attack, and how does it affect DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
A supply chain attack compromises software or dependencies from trusted sources (e.g., third-party
libraries, public images). It affects DevSecOps by introducing vulnerabilities in otherwise secure
environments. Preventative measures include scanning dependencies and enforcing policies on
trusted sources.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is container orchestration, and how do you secure it?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
Container orchestration refers to managing the deployment, scaling, and operations of containers. To
secure it:
• Use RBAC to control access to resources.
DevOps Shack
• Implement network policies for pod communication.
• Regularly patch the orchestration platform (e.g., Kubernetes).

DevSecOps & Security.question: How do you secure microservices architecture?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
• Use mutual TLS for service-to-service communication.
• Implement authentication and authorization (e.g., OAuth2, JWT).
• Regularly scan microservices for vulnerabilities.
• Implement rate limiting and monitoring for malicious activities.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is mutual TLS (mTLS), and how does it enhance security in microservices?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
Mutual TLS (mTLS) ensures that both the client and server authenticate each other using certificates
before establishing a connection. It enhances security by encrypting communications and verifying
the identity of services.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is a service mesh, and how does it help with security in DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
A service mesh (e.g., Istio, Linkerd) manages microservices communications by providing
observability, security, and traffic management. Security benefits include automatic mTLS
encryption, policy enforcement, and service-level authentication/authorization.

DevSecOps & Security.question: How does using multi-factor authentication (MFA) enhance security in DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
MFA requires users to provide two or more verification factors to gain access, reducing the risk of
compromised credentials being used to access critical resources. This is essential for securing CI/CD
pipelines, infrastructure, and sensitive environments.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is Zero Trust, and how does it apply to DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
Zero Trust is a security model that assumes no trust in the network or devices and requires
continuous authentication and verification at every step. In DevSecOps, it applies to restricting
access to services and infrastructure, enforcing least privilege, and continuously monitoring for
anomalies.

DevSecOps & Security.question: How do you handle vulnerability management in DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
DevOps Shack
• Implement continuous vulnerability scanning tools like Trivy or SonarQube.
• Automate patching and updates.
• Ensure timely response to vulnerability alerts by integrating them into the CI/CD pipeline.
• Use vulnerability management dashboards for visibility and tracking.

DevSecOps & Security.question: How does the concept of “Security as Code” work in DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
Security as Code refers to embedding security policies and configurations directly into the code and
automation pipelines. This allows security checks to be automated, repeatable, and version-
controlled, ensuring consistent security practices across environments.

DevSecOps & Security.question: How do you secure APIs in a microservices architecture?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
To secure APIs:
• Implement strong authentication and authorization using OAuth2 or JWT.
• Use API gateways for rate limiting, throttling, and logging.
• Encrypt data in transit with TLS.
• Validate input to prevent injection attacks.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is a security pipeline, and how is it set up in DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
A security pipeline automates security checks throughout the CI/CD lifecycle. It includes stages like
SAST, DAST, dependency scanning, container scanning, and policy enforcement. Tools like Jenkins,
GitLab CI, or Azure Pipelines integrate these security tools into the development workflow.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is the role of logging and monitoring in DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
Logging and monitoring are essential for detecting and responding to security incidents in real-time.
They provide visibility into system behavior and anomalies. Tools like Prometheus, Grafana, and ELK
(Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana) are commonly used for monitoring, while alerts are set up for
abnormal activities.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is DevSecOps in the context of cloud-native applications?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
In cloud-native applications, DevSecOps integrates security into containerized environments,
microservices, and serverless architectures. It involves securing CI/CD pipelines, applying network
policies, managing secrets, and continuously monitoring the dynamic infrastructure.

DevSecOps & Security.question: How do you manage security in serverless functions?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
• Use minimal IAM permissions for the function.
• Secure the event triggers (e.g., API Gateway) using authentication mechanisms.
• Regularly scan serverless functions for vulnerabilities.
• Monitor and log function activity using services like AWS CloudWatch or Azure Monitor.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What are security groups in AWS, and how do you use them in DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
Security groups act as virtual firewalls for EC2 instances, controlling inbound and outbound traffic. In
DevSecOps, security groups are configured using IaC tools like Terraform, ensuring they are correctly
set to allow only the necessary traffic.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is a container registry, and how do you secure it?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
A container registry stores container images for deployment. To secure it:
• Enable image signing and scanning.
• Use private registries to restrict access.
• Enforce least privilege on access permissions.
• Regularly update and scan images for vulnerabilities.
DevOps Shack

DevSecOps & Security.question: How do you ensure data privacy in DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
• Encrypt sensitive data in transit and at rest.
• Implement proper access controls and audit logging.
• Use data masking or tokenization for sensitive information.
• Regularly audit access to sensitive data.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What are the key features of HashiCorp Vault?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
HashiCorp Vault provides:
• Secure secret storage.
• Dynamic secrets (e.g., AWS, database credentials).
• Encryption as a service.
• Role-based access control (RBAC) for secret access.
• Auditing and monitoring of secret usage.

DevSecOps & Security.question: How do you implement continuous compliance in DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
Continuous compliance ensures that systems and code adhere to security and regulatory standards
throughout the CI/CD process. This can be achieved using tools like Puppet, Chef Inspec, or
Terraform Sentinel, which automatically enforce compliance rules.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is the significance of “Immutable Infrastructure” in DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
Immutable Infrastructure refers to infrastructure that cannot be modified once deployed. Changes
require creating new infrastructure rather than modifying the existing one, reducing the risk of
configuration drift and unauthorized changes, thus enhancing security.

DevSecOps & Security.question: How does TLS work in ensuring security in DevSecOps pipelines?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
TLS (Transport Layer Security) ensures encrypted communication between services or between the
CI/CD pipeline and other systems. It prevents data interception and man-in-the-middle attacks by
encrypting data during transmission and verifying the server’s identity using certificates.
DevOps Shack

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is a security policy in DevSecOps, and how do you enforce it?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
A security policy defines the rules and configurations that protect systems and data. In DevSecOps,
policies can be enforced automatically through IaC tools (Terraform), security automation tools (Chef
Inspec), or security gateways (WAFs).

DevSecOps & Security.question: How do you secure multi-cloud environments in DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
• Apply consistent security policies across all cloud providers.
• Use centralized identity and access management (IAM) solutions.
• Encrypt data in transit and at rest.
• Implement cross-cloud monitoring and logging tools.
• Regularly audit cloud configurations for misconfigurations.

DevSecOps & Security.question: How do you handle secrets rotation in DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
Secrets rotation involves updating secrets (e.g., API keys, passwords) regularly to limit the exposure
window. This can be automated using tools like HashiCorp Vault or AWS Secrets Manager, which
dynamically generate and rotate secrets without human intervention.

DevSecOps & Security.question: How do you perform compliance checks for containerized applications?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
Use tools like Aqua Security or Twistlock to automatically scan containers for compliance with
security policies and best practices. These tools also provide audit logs and reports that help
demonstrate compliance with industry standards (e.g., CIS Benchmarks).

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is the difference between DevSecOps and traditional security?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
Traditional security is often applied at the end of the development lifecycle, leading to bottlenecks.
DevSecOps integrates security throughout the SDLC, automating security testing and ensuring
continuous compliance without slowing down the development process.

DevSecOps & Security.question: How do you implement Zero Trust in DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
• Implement strong identity verification for all users and systems.
DevOps Shack
• Enforce least privilege access.
• Continuously monitor and audit system access.
• Encrypt all communications using TLS or VPNs.
• Use multi-factor authentication (MFA) for access control.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is container drift, and how do you detect it?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
Container drift occurs when a container’s state changes after deployment (e.g., installing new
software). It introduces security risks as it deviates from the intended state. Drift can be detected
using tools like Docker Bench or Kubernetes monitoring tools.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What are CIS Benchmarks, and how are they used in DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
CIS Benchmarks are industry-accepted security standards that provide best practices for securing
systems and applications. In DevSecOps, these benchmarks can be enforced using automated tools
like Aqua or Chef Inspec to ensure systems remain compliant.

DevSecOps & Security.question: How do you secure cloud-native applications using Istio?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
Istio enhances security by:
• Enabling mutual TLS (mTLS) for service-to-service communication.
• Enforcing policies for authentication and authorization.
• Securing ingress and egress traffic using an API gateway.
• Providing end-to-end encryption for sensitive data.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What are the key considerations for implementing a WAF in a DevSecOps pipeline?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
A Web Application Firewall (WAF) can be integrated into the DevSecOps pipeline to protect web
applications. Considerations include:
• Automating WAF policy updates based on new threats.
• Monitoring WAF logs for anomalous traffic.
• Testing WAF configurations as part of the CI/CD process.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is a dynamic secret in HashiCorp Vault, and why is it important?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
DevOps Shack
A dynamic secret is generated on-demand and is short-lived. It minimizes the risk of credential
exposure by providing credentials that are valid only for a specific period, after which they expire
automatically. It’s useful for temporary database access or API keys.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is network segmentation, and how does it help in DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
Network segmentation involves dividing a network into smaller, isolated segments to limit access to
critical systems. In DevSecOps, segmentation can prevent lateral movement within the network,
reducing the impact of a potential security breach.

DevSecOps & Security.question: How do you monitor microservices in a DevSecOps environment?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
• Use tools like Prometheus and Grafana for metrics and alerting.
• Enable distributed tracing with Jaeger or Zipkin.
• Implement centralized logging using the ELK stack.
• Set up dashboards and alerts for performance or security anomalies.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is a policy engine, and how is it used in DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
A policy engine enforces security and compliance policies automatically in DevSecOps pipelines.
Tools like OPA (Open Policy Agent) or Terraform Sentinel check for policy violations in code,
infrastructure, or configuration changes before deployment.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is a compliance-as-code framework, and how does it work in DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
Compliance-as-code ensures compliance policies are written as code and integrated into the CI/CD
pipeline. It automates checks and enforcement, ensuring the infrastructure and applications meet
compliance requirements without manual intervention.

DevSecOps & Security.question: How does the MITRE ATT&CK framework apply to DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
The MITRE ATT&CK framework provides a knowledge base of tactics, techniques, and procedures
(TTPs) used by adversaries. In DevSecOps, it can be used to model potential attacks and integrate
defenses, such as automated alerts for suspicious behaviors.
DevOps Shack

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is OWASP ZAP, and how does it integrate with DevSecOps pipelines?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
OWASP ZAP (Zed Attack Proxy) is a DAST tool that tests web applications for security vulnerabilities.
It can be integrated into CI/CD pipelines to automatically scan applications for issues like XSS, SQL
injection, and insecure configurations before deployment.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is role-based access control (RBAC), and how do you implement it in cloud environments?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
RBAC restricts user access based on roles. In cloud environments like AWS or Azure, it’s implemented
by creating roles with defined permissions and assigning them to users or services. This ensures
users have only the access they need to perform specific tasks.

DevSecOps & Security.question: How do you implement DLP (Data Loss Prevention) in DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
Data Loss Prevention (DLP) can be implemented by:
• Encrypting sensitive data at rest and in transit.
• Setting up data access policies and audit trails.
• Monitoring for anomalous data transfers.
• Using DLP tools like Symantec DLP or McAfee DLP.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is a container escape, and how do you prevent it?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
A container escape occurs when an attacker breaks out of a container’s isolation and gains access to
the host system. Prevention techniques include:
• Running containers with the least privilege.
• Securing the host OS with patches and updates.
• Enabling container security features like AppArmor or SELinux.
DevOps Shack

DevSecOps & Security.question: How do you implement encryption in a CI/CD pipeline?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
Encryption in CI/CD pipelines can be implemented by:
• Encrypting environment variables and secrets.
• Using tools like GPG to encrypt sensitive files or code.
• Ensuring that data in transit (e.g., between Jenkins and the server) is encrypted using TLS.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is TLS termination, and why is it important for security?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
TLS termination refers to decrypting incoming TLS (HTTPS) traffic at a load balancer or proxy before
passing it to internal services. It ensures secure communication between external clients and the
entry point to your services while offloading the decryption overhead from backend servers.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is an RASP tool, and how does it help in DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
RASP (Runtime Application Self-Protection) tools detect and prevent attacks in real-time by
monitoring an application’s behavior at runtime. They can automatically block malicious actions or
inject defenses against known vulnerabilities, enhancing security during production.

DevSecOps & Security.question: How do you secure communication between microservices?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
• Use mTLS for encrypted and authenticated communication.
• Implement API gateways for centralized security policies.
• Use JWT or OAuth2 for service-to-service authentication.
• Apply network segmentation and security policies to control traffic.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is a penetration test, and how does it differ from vulnerability scanning in DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
A penetration test simulates real-world attacks to identify security weaknesses, whereas vulnerability
scanning is an automated process that detects known vulnerabilities. Penetration testing is more
thorough, often involving manual testing and exploitation techniques.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is application hardening in DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
DevOps Shack
Application hardening involves reducing the attack surface of an application by:
• Disabling unnecessary features and services.
• Applying security patches regularly.
• Securing configurations (e.g., strong authentication, encryption).
• Minimizing open network ports.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is the role of identity and access management (IAM) in DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
IAM controls who can access what resources. In DevSecOps, IAM ensures that only authorized users
and services have access to infrastructure, applications, and sensitive data. Proper IAM
configurations, such as least privilege and MFA, protect against unauthorized access.

DevSecOps & Security.question: How do you secure container orchestration platforms like Kubernetes?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
• Use RBAC to restrict access to resources.
• Enforce network policies for pod communication.
• Regularly scan and patch Kubernetes components.
• Enable mTLS for pod-to-pod communication.
• Use secrets management tools for sensitive data.

DevSecOps & Security.question: How do you secure CI/CD pipelines in a multi-cloud environment?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
• Implement centralized identity management across clouds.
• Use cloud-native security services (e.g., AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault) for secrets
management.
• Regularly audit and apply consistent security policies across clouds.
• Encrypt data in transit and at rest.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is the difference between a public and private container registry?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
A public registry is accessible to anyone, while a private registry restricts access to specific users or
systems. Private registries are more secure as they control access to sensitive or proprietary
container images, reducing the risk of exposure to vulnerabilities.
DevOps Shack

DevSecOps & Security.question: How do you implement security logging and monitoring for containers?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
• Enable logging for container runtime activities.
• Use centralized logging tools like Fluentd and ELK for aggregating logs.
• Monitor container metrics using Prometheus or Grafana.
• Set up alerts for unusual container behavior or resource spikes.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is the role of a container security scanner in DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
A container security scanner detects vulnerabilities in container images by comparing them to known
vulnerability databases. Tools like Trivy or Clair can be integrated into the CI/CD pipeline to
automatically scan images before they are deployed.

DevSecOps & Security.question: What is an infrastructure penetration test, and how does it help in DevSecOps?
DevSecOps & Security.answer:
Infrastructure penetration testing simulates attacks against cloud or on-premise infrastructure to
identify security vulnerabilities. It helps teams proactively discover and mitigate weaknesses in the
network, systems, and configurations.
